summary: Graham argues that most addictions are concentrated, technologically improved versions of milder predecessors, and that this concentration is accelerating. The same progress that yields benefits also makes more things intensely engaging, pushing people to live increasingly “abnormal” lives if they want to function well. Because social customs adapt slowly, individuals will have to proactively decide what to avoid and set personal boundaries.
key_points: Technological progress concentrates what we want, and that concentration often turns pleasures into addictions.
The pace of new, more addictive products is accelerating, outstripping the slow evolution of social norms.
As addictiveness rises, statistical normality diverges from what is healthy or productive.
Customs and laws can mitigate addiction, but they lag and have costs, so personal strategies become essential.
Avoidance will increasingly define people who want to stay effective amid pervasive, evolving temptations.
money_quotes: The world is more addictive than it was 40 years ago.
You can probably take it as a rule of thumb from now on that if people don't think you're weird, you're living badly.
Unless we want to be canaries in the coal mine of each new addiction—the people whose sad example becomes a lesson to future generations—we'll have to figure out for ourselves what to avoid and how.
I've avoided most addictions, but the Internet got me because it became addictive while I was using it.
We'll increasingly be defined by what we say no to.
key_insight: As technology rapidly intensifies what we like, social norms cannot keep up, so living well will increasingly require deliberate, sometimes eccentric, personal limits.
